coredata.dc:title: Photoelectrochemical quenching-recovery biosensor based on NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3 for the detection of trypsin
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Ying
Zhang, Chenning
Bai, Shuru
Su, Jiaxue
Zhou, Xunyong
Zhao, Longshan
coredata.dc:description:
Background The content of trypsin will change when pancreatic diseases occur, therefore developing a high-performance method for trypsin detection is of great significance for guiding patients on medication plans and improving their prognosis. Photoelectrochemical (PEC) analysis techniques have emerged as a solution to apply for bioassays.
Results Herein, the Fe2O3@Bi2S3 and Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (NSCQDs) were successfully synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Subsequently, NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3 with a photocurrent amplification effect covered on fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) electrode as the substrate material and apoferritin (APO) as a bio-recognition element to quench the photocurrent of the substrate material which can be excited with light. Due to the decomposition specifically between APO and trypsin, the photocurrent response increased. The linear range for trypsin detection showed satisfied results from 2 to 1000 ng mL−1 under optimal conditions, with a detection limit of 0.42 ng mL−1 and a recovery rate of 97.41 %–103.02 %, enabling efficient quantitative analysis of trypsin.
Significance In this experiment, a PEC biosensor with simple operation, low detection limit, excellent selectivity and strong stability was successfully prepared, enabling quantitative analysis of trypsin in human serum samples through the quenching-recovery mechanism. It holds great significance for diagnosis and serves as a practical method for the detection of trypsin in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectrochemical
Biosensor
Trypsin
Detection
Quenching
Recovery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism of photocurrent formation and synthesis analysis of PEC biosensor
Optimization of experimental conditions for the synthesis of PEC biosensor
Stability, reproducibility and selectivity of the PEC biosensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University
Youqing lift Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Youqing lift Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University [ YQ202206 ] and the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University [ ZQN2016011 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image of Fe2O3@Bi2S3 (A, C, D); TEM image of Fe2O3@Bi2S3 (B); EDS spectra of Fe2O3@Bi2S3 (E); XRD image of Fe2O3@ Bi2S3 (F).
The XPS spectroscopy of NSCQDs (A); The high resolution XPS of O 1s (B), N 1s (C), C 1s (D).
The XPS spectroscopy of NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3 (A); The high resolution XPS of O 1s (B), N 1s (C), C 1s (D) Bi 4f (E), Fe 2p (F).
Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of Fe2O3@Bi2S3 and NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3.
The band gaps of Fe2O3 and Bi2S3 (A–B); Mott-Shottky plots of Fe2O3 and Bi2S3 (C–D).
Schematic diagram of photoelectric response of NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3.

EIS response with different modifications (A); Photocurrent response under different modifications (B) (a: FTO; b: Fe2O3@Bi2S3/FTO; c: NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3/FTO; d: APO/NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3/FTO; e: Trypsin/APO/NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3/FTO).

Optimization of conditions: optimization of the amount of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (A); optimization of the concentration of NSCQDs (B); optimization of the incubation time of NSCQDs (C); optimization of the concentration of AA (D); optimization of the concentration of APO (E); optimization of the incubation time of APO (F).

Photocurrent response of the sensing platform at 400 s under several on/off irradiation cycles (A); Photocurrent response of the sensing platform after a long period of 75 d (B); Reproducibility of the sensing platform (C); Effect of different pH (5-10) on the sensing platform (D).
Photocurrent reaction of Trypsin at different concentrations (2–1000 ng mL−1) (A); Linearity of this sensing platform (B).
Construction of NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3/FTO platform for sensitive detection of trypsin.
Comparison between the methods reported in other literature and the present study.
Detection of trypsin in serum samples.

head.ce:title: Photoelectrochemical quenching-recovery biosensor based on NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3 for the detection of trypsin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, Shenyang, Liaoning, 110016, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: HC Enzyme Biotech. Co. Ltd, Shenzhen, Guangdong, 518001, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this paper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The content of trypsin will change when pancreatic diseases occur, therefore developing a high-performance method for trypsin detection is of great significance for guiding patients on medication plans and improving their prognosis. Photoelectrochemical (PEC) analysis techniques have emerged as a solution to apply for bioassays.

Herein, the Fe2O3@Bi2S3 and Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (NSCQDs) were successfully synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Subsequently, NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3 with a photocurrent amplification effect covered on fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) electrode as the substrate material and apoferritin (APO) as a bio-recognition element to quench the photocurrent of the substrate material which can be excited with light. Due to the decomposition specifically between APO and trypsin, the photocurrent response increased. The linear range for trypsin detection showed satisfied results from 2 to 1000 ng mL−1 under optimal conditions, with a detection limit of 0.42 ng mL−1 and a recovery rate of 97.41 %–103.02 %, enabling efficient quantitative analysis of trypsin.

In this experiment, a PEC biosensor with simple operation, low detection limit, excellent selectivity and strong stability was successfully prepared, enabling quantitative analysis of trypsin in human serum samples through the quenching-recovery mechanism. It holds great significance for diagnosis and serves as a practical method for the detection of trypsin in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Trypsin is one of the most important pancreatic digestive enzymes which originally from the activation of trypsinogen, and is involved in controlling many physiological and pathological processes [1]. Usually, trypsin catalyzes the hydrolytic cleavage of specific peptide bonds to facilitate protein absorption [2]. However, many diseases such as pancreatitis, cystic fibrosis, cancer and neurodegenerative disorders may occur when trypsin activity is altered [3–6]. Hence, it is highly desirable to develop simple and sensitive methods to quantify the concentrations of trypsin. Many traditional techniques have made some contributions to the development of trypsin detection including fluorescence assay, electrogenerated chemiluminescence, electrochemical method and enzyme-linked immunosorbent Assay [7–10]. However, the low sensitivity and detection efficiency make those assays face some difficulties. Therefore, improving a more simple, sensitive, and selective trypsin detection method remains a crucial mission.

PEC analysis combines the photoelectric conversion process with the electrochemical process and has gained a lot of attention in the detection of biological samples due to the low background noise, high sensitivity, and simple operation [11,12]. The performance of PEC sensors depended directly on photoactive materials used in the field of PEC sensing. Metal ions and semiconductor materials were the most common photoactive materials considering their excellent photoelectric properties [13–15]. Bi2S3 has been widely studied as an important and attractive PEC semiconductor material [16,17]. The narrow band gap of Bi2S3 enables full utilization of both ultraviolet light and visible light. However, the rapid recombination between electrons and holes limits the photoactivity of Bi2S3 [18,19]. Fe2O3 is an inexpensive and environmentally friendly visible-light-driven catalyst that can be used in many optical fields [20]. However, pure Fe2O3 exhibits certain limitations, such as low carrier mobility, short lifetimes for a few carriers, and limited diffusion lengths [21]. To overcome these limitations, we use a simple hydrothermal synthesis method to synthesize Fe2O3@Bi2S3 heterojunction, which can reduce the electron-hole recombination rate and increase diffusion length to greatly improve the photocurrent response. Given the non-toxicity, biocompatibility, high stability, and good light absorption capacity, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are also highly regarded [22]. When carbon quantum dots are combined with other photoactive semiconductor materials, the electron transfer rate and light absorption capacity can be improved [23]. Previous research works have demonstrated that after doping atomic such as nitrogen and sulfur to CQDs, the optical performance can be further improved [24–26]. We attempt to make nitrogen and sulfur carbon quantum dots (NSCQDs) absorbed on the surface of Fe2O3@Bi2S3 to further improve the optical properties of Fe2O3@Bi2S3 heterojunction.

To achieve the quantitative detection of trypsin, a quenching-recovery mechanism was used. Specifically, apoferritin (APO) was a protein-based biomaterial that can adhere to the surface of photoactive materials, resulting in a severe inhibition of photocurrent response due to the insulating effect of the protein [27]. Whereas, the presence of trypsin effectively decomposed APO and restored the photocurrent [28]. Heavy chain subunits (H-subunit) and light chain subunits (L-subunit) are the two subunits of APO composition in mammalian [29]. APO with a larger content of L-subunits are cationic ferritins with alkaline isoelectric points. In contrast, those with a higher content of H-subunits are termed acidic ferritins [30]. The NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3 prepared in the experiment was negatively charged with a zeta potential of −21.4 mV (Fig. S1). To construct the photoelectrochemical biosensor, immobilizing APO on the surface of NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3 through electrostatic adsorption, horse spleen APO rich in L-subunits with a theoretical isoelectric point of 9.40 was used for the experiment. Record the increased value of photocurrent after hydrolysis of APO by trypsin, the concentration of trypsin was determined. Ultimately, the photocurrent generated by trypsin exhibited a linear increase in the range of 2–1000 ng mL−1 with an ultralow detection limit of 0.42 ng mL−1. To the best of our knowledge, this might be the first report on the PEC biosensor based on NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3 photoactive materials, providing a reliable trypsin detection strategy in human serum samples utilizing the specific binding between APO and trypsin. Consequently, such high specificity and sensitivity PEC platform shows great potential application in practical diagnosis.
Detailed information on materials and apparatus was placed in supporting information.

The synthesis method of Fe2O3@Bi2S3 was an improvement on the previous methods [31]. 0.31 g (NH2)2CS was first completely dissolved in 25 mL ultrapure water, then 1.02 g Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was added and stirred vigorously at a temperature of 60 °C for 15 min, 0.2 g Fe(NO3)3·9H2O was added to the above solution after stirring. After that, the resulting mixed solution was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 20 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the product was repeatedly washed with anhydrous ethanol and ultrapure water and was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. Finally, the centrifuged product was transferred to an oven for drying at 60 °C for 8 h to obtain Fe2O3@Bi2S3 and stored at room temperature for subsequent operation.

NSCQDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method in this study according to the previously reported method with a slight modification [32]. First, 0.21 g ammonium citrate and 0.23 g (NH2)2CS were dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water, the mixed solution was stirred violently for 10 min. Then the product was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave and placed in an autoclave at 160 °C for 4 h. At last, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, and the obtained NSCQDs were taken out and stored at 4 °C for subsequent operation.

The FTO electrode was cut into 2.5 × 2.5 cm2 slices and cleaned with acetone, ultrapure water, and ethanol for 30 min successively. Subsequently, 40 μL of Fe2O3@Bi2S3 (10 mg mL−1) was added to the FTO, and after drying at 60 °C for 8 h, the same volume of NSCQDs (15 mg mL−1) was incubated on the resulting electrode at 60 °C for 2 h to form a NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3 electrode. Finally, 1.4 mg mL−1 APO with 20 μL was dropped onto the modified electrode at 50 °C for 3 h to obtain an APO/NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3/FTO electrode (Scheme 1 ). The prepared electrode was rinsed with 0.1 mol L−1 PBS buffer, left to dry naturally and stored at 4 °C for further work.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) test, mott-schottky (M − S) plots and photocurrent signals were measured on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation. A three-electrode system was applied in the current research: the prepared electrodes were used as the working electrode; the Ag/AgCl electrode was served as the reference electrode; and the platinum electrode was utilized as the counter electrode. The excitation light was a 250 W xenon lamp, switched on and off every 10 s, and a voltage of 0.0 V was applied. The serum sample that we used from the First Affiliated Hospital of Jilin University and approved by the Human Ethics Committee. Take Ascorbic acid (AA) and trypsin into PBS buffer for the test solution.

SEM and TEM images of Fe2O3@Bi2S3 heterostructure showed that Fe2O3@Bi2S3 consisted of about 500 nm clustered flower-like structure composed of a large number of tiny nanorods with diameter ranges from 30 to 60 nm and the average length was about 1–2 μm (Fig. 1 A–C). To further confirm the composition of the obtained Fe2O3@Bi2S3 nanocomposites, mapping elements were applied, and it can be confirmed that S, O, Fe and Bi elements co-exist and are evenly dispersed in the nanoflower (Fig. 1D). EDS scanning analysis showed that the sample Fe2O3@Bi2S3 was mainly composed of Bi and S elements with a stoichiometric ratio of 2:3, which was very close to the value of pure Bi2S3 (Fig. 1E).

According to the XRD pattern of Fe2O3@Bi2S3 in Fig. 1F, the main Bi2S3 phase was found, which was accompanied by the diffraction peaks of cannonite phase impurity attributed to the monoclinic phase of Bi2O(OH)2(SO4) [33]. The main peaks of Fe2O3@Bi2S3 can be easily indexed to the orthogonal Bi2S3 structure (JCPDS card no.00-017-0320) with the main peaks related to the (020), (130), (211) and (221) planes with unit-cell constants of a = 11.15 Å, b = 11.30 Å and c = 3.97 Å [34]. For the prepared Fe2O3@Bi2S3 heterostructure, only the orthogonal Bi2S3 phase was detected, which can be attributed to the high dispersion of Fe2O3, poor crystallinity or its content in the heterogeneous structure being lower than the XRD detection limit. Besides, in the Fe2O3@Bi2S3 sample, the position of the diffraction peaks did not change significantly, nor did the peaks become wider, however, the major diffraction peak intensity of pure Bi2S3 was weakened, suggesting that Fe2O3 may play a role in the redirection of Bi2S3 crystal growth.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to reflect the elemental composition and chemical bonding between the elements of NSCQDs in this experiment. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the wide full scan XPS spectrum pointed out four characteristic peaks at 533 eV, 400 eV, 286 eV and 162 eV corresponded to O 1s, N 1s, C 1s and S 2p respectively, which can prove the existence of four elements including C, N, O and S [35]. In addition, a peak of 532.2 eV can be seen in the high-resolution O 1s spectrum, confirming the presence of the CO bond (Fig. 2B) [36]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum consisted of three peaks with binding energies around 398.6 eV, 399.3 eV and 401 eV belonging to CN, N–H and C–N–C, respectively (Fig. 2C) [37]. Fig. 2D displayed the high-resolution C 1s spectrum, where 284.8 eV, 287.1 eV, and 288.8 eV were associated with C–C, C–S, and C–O, respectively [38]. Those results indicated that amino and carboxyl functional groups on the surface of NSCQDs.

To further investigate the surface function groups of NSCQDs in this experiment, the FT-IR spectrometer technique was performed (Fig. S2A). The peak at 3170 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibration of N–H/O–H, the characteristic absorption peak at 2060 cm−1 can be judged as the out-of-plane bending vibration of C–H, and the absorption peak at 1097 cm−1 can be identified as the stretching vibration of CS [39]. The characteristic absorption peak at 1703 cm−1 and 1635 cm−1 can be attributed to the carbonyl function groups [40]. The characteristic absorption at 1577 cm−1 and 1414 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CC and C–N respectively [41]. The above result confirmed that NSCQDs with a large number of iconic functional groups such as amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. Consequently, as shown in Fig. S2B, NSCQDs had a relatively obvious absorption peak spectrum in the ultraviolet region, and its characteristic absorption light centered at 262 nm and 324 nm were related to the π→π* transition of CC band and the n→π* transition of CO/COOH band, respectively [42]. Moreover, under the irradiation of a 365 nm UV lamp, NSCQDs exhibited strong blue fluorescence in the upper right.

The chemical composition of NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3 was investigated by the XPS technique and the results were shown in Fig. 3 A. After the deposition of NSCQDs, the XPS peaks showed that NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3 contained not only Fe, O, S, Bi, C and N elements, but also the Sn element. The signals of Sn element might come from the FTO substrate. Typical high resolution XPS spectra of O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, Bi 4f and Fe 2p of the NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3 were presented in Fig. 3B–F. The peak with binding energies of 531.51 eV in Fig. 3B was classical metal carbonate bonds, which was provided by the binding of carboxyl groups in the NSCQDs to metal atoms in Fe2O3@Bi2S3 [43,44]. In comparison with NSCQDs, the positions of the N 1s and C 1s peaks were not essentially changed (Fig. 3C and D). Furthermore, the Bi 4f peaks at 158.96 eV and 164.28 eV were classical Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 in oxide, consisting of O in Fe2O3 and Bi in Bi2S3 (Fig. 3E) [45,46]. The peak at 162.18 eV can be attributed to the binding energies of Bi 4f5/2 in Bi2S3 [47]. Two peaks that appeared at 711.60 eV and 725.77 eV in Fig. 3F came from Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 peaks of Fe3+, respectively [48,49]. The above results showed that NSCQDs had been successfully doped into the Fe2O3@Bi2S3.

To further analyze the structure of the prepared NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3, nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms measurement was performed in the study to determine the relevant specific surface area and the pore size (Tables S1–2). As shown in Fig. 4 , The isotherms of Fe2O3@Bi2S3 showed linear absorption in the low range of relative pressure, while the curves exhibited a small hysteresis loop in the high relative pressure range, suggesting that the presence of abundant mesoporous structures in the architectures according to IUPAC classification [50,51]. The presence of mesoporous structure makes it easier to bind to NSCQDs that contain a large number of carboxyl groups, which was consistent with the performance of the XPS results [52]. In addition, it can be seen from Table S1 that when NSCQDs were incubated on Fe2O3@Bi2S3, the specific surface area decreased, which may be a result of the small particle size of NSCQDs entering into the large mesopores of Fe2O3@Bi2S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of photocurrent formation and synthesis analysis of PEC biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To predict the charge transfer mechanism of NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3, UV–visible diffuse reflection and M − S plots were performed. The UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectrograms in Fig. S3 were used to assess the optical absorption abilities of as-prepared NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3. The absorption edge of the pure Fe2O3 is at around 590 nm, consistent with the reported value [53]. After the addition of Bi2S3, the absorption edge of Fe2O3@Bi2S3 extended to the full visible region. In addition, with further modification of NSCQDs, the light absorption ability compared to Fe2O3@Bi2S3 exhibited enhanced light absorption in the ultraviolet and infrared regions. The energy band gap (Eg) of Fe2O3 and Bi2S3 can be achieved through the tauc equation (ahν)2 = A (hν-Eg) [54,55]. The band gap energies of Fe2O3 and Bi2S3 were estimated based on the obtained tauc plots to be 2.24 eV and 1.32 eV, respectively (Fig. 5 A and B). The calculated results were consistent with the previously reported Bi2S3 and Fe2O3 [56–58]

M − S plots were applied to determine the CB and VB positions of Fe2O3 and Bi2S3. As depicted in Fig. 5C and D, the flat-band potential of Fe2O3 and Bi2S3 could be confirmed to be −0.21 (−0.01 eV vs. NHE) and −0.42 eV (−0.22 eV vs. NHE), respectively. The CB potential in the n-type semiconductor is 0.1–0.3 eV lower than the flat-band potential [59,60]. Therefore, the ECB of Fe2O3 and Bi2S3 can be estimated as −0.11 eV and −0.32 eV. According to the formula EVB = Eg + ECB, the corresponding EVB of Fe2O3 and Bi2S3 were about 2.13 eV and 1.00 eV, respectively [61,62].

The mechanism of photocurrent generation in NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3 can be illustrated in Fig. 6 . With irradiation, electrons belonging to photoactive materials transition from the VB to the CB, forming electron-hole pairs [63]. NSCQDs with good optical properties can enhance the light absorption of Fe2O3@Bi2S3. As a result, more photogenerated electrons and photogenerated holes can be produced from the NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3 composites in the presence of light [64]. Photoelectrons from Bi2S3 can be injected into the CB of Fe2O3 and transferred to FTO to obtain a photocurrent that can reflect the change of detection substance [65]. At the same time, the photogenerated holes in Fe2O3 can be transferred to the VB of Bi2S3 and then moved to the surface of the material, whereafter it can be consumed efficiently by AA, a common electron donor and photocurrent amplifier [66,67]. These results indicated that the excitation and separation effects were significantly enhanced, leading to an extension in the lifetime of photogenerated carriers and an enhancement in the PEC properties.

To further verify the successful synthesis of PEC biosensors, EIS measurements were carried out on the PEC sensing platform. As shown in Fig. 7 A, the diameter of the semicircular part at high frequency represents the electron transfer resistance (Rct), which can illustrate the electron transfer confinement process. Compared to the bare FTO electrode, the Rct value of the electrode (curve b) decreased due to the attachment of Fe2O3@Bi2S3 heterojunction to the electrode promoting the transfers of electrons. After coating NSCQDs on the electrode (curve c), the Rct decreased further, which can be attributed to the good electrical conductivity of NSCQDs and the electron transfer rate increased. However, after the surface of the fabricated electrode was modified with APO and trypsin, the Rct of the electrodes (curves d-e) began to increase accordingly. This was because the diffusion of electrons and holes to the electrode surface was hindered by the large steric hindrance of protein molecules [68].

As seen in Fig. 7B, with the gradual introduction of Fe2O3@Bi2S3, NSCQDs and APO, the photocurrent increased sequentially and then decreased, which was in response to the results of EIS. However, when the fabricated electrode was modified with trypsin, the photocurrent was enhanced, which can be distinguishable from the results of EIS. It can be seen as a result of APO being decomposed by trypsin [69]. In summary, according to the changes in Rct values of the modified electrodes in the above different steps, the results indicated that the PEC biosensor platform has been successfully prepared at each step.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions for the synthesis of PEC biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The substance content and incubation time were crucial for the performance of the designed biosensor. We optimized the relevant conditions and the response results of photocurrent were discussed in Fig. 8 .

Fig. 8A showed the photocurrent signal under different content of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O as the raw material for the synthesis of Fe2O3. On increasing the content of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, there was an increase in photocurrent response up to 0.20 g, followed by a decrease in the 0.2–0.25 g content range because electron transfer was hindered. Therefore, 0.2 g was chosen as the optimum condition in the following experiment.

For the incubation concentration and time of NSCQDs, as shown in Fig. 8B and C, the signal amplified with the concentration of NSCQDs and then declined after 15 mg mL−1 because of the enhanced electron recombination effect. The photocurrent reached the maximum in the incubation time of 2 h and reached a plateau in 2–7 h, indicating NSCQDs completely adhere to the surface of Fe2O3@Bi2S3 heterojunction. As a result, 15 mg L−1 NSCQDs incubated for 2 h were selected as the optimal condition for NSCQDs.

As can be seen from Fig. 8D, the photocurrent reached the maximum when the AA concentration at 1.4 mg mL−1 and reached a plateau at 1.4–1.8 mg mL−1. The phenomenon could be caused by the saturation of the electron donor [70].

From Fig. 8E, we can obtain the optimal incubation time and concentration of APO. The photocurrent decreased as the concentration of APO increased from 0 to 1.4 mg mL−1. Subsequently, the photocurrent reached the platform period. The photocurrent decreased with the increase of incubation time, reaching a minimum at 3 h as shown in Fig. 8F. The above results showed that APO formed on the photoactive material reached saturation and inhibited the photocurrent effect to the maximum extent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, reproducibility and selectivity of the PEC biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the substrate material was estimated from the analysis of the prepared PEC biosensor. As shown in Fig. 9 A, the stability of Fe2O3@Bi2S3 as the substrate material was stable without any noticeable changes after multiple light on and off experiments within 400 s in this study. In addition, we evaluated the long-term stability of the substrate material by storing the substrate material in a wet state in a refrigerator at 4 °C for 75 days, and the detection frequency was 15 days once (Fig. 9B). Ultimately, the result indicated the short-term and long-term stability of the substrate material was excellent and well anchored on the FTO. Furthermore, pH was a critical factor in ensuring the stability of the sensor platform. The effect of different pH solutions on the study was investigated (Fig. 9D) and we found that the biosensor maintained a satisfactory photocurrent response over the pH range of 7–9, indicating its viability for bioassay.

To evaluate the reproducibility of the PEC biosensor, trypsin at a concentration of 6 ng mL−1 was repeatedly detected five times, and the obtained photocurrent response was without significant change, demonstrating that the biosensor had good reproducibility (Fig. 9C).

Selectivity was a key metric for assessing the potential value of a biosensor in an application. In the article, the specific binding between trypsin and APO allowed the determination of the trypsin content and the presence of other proteolytic enzymes in human serum may interfere with the results. Therefore, the selectivity experiments were performed in the following common enzymes that are readily found in human serum at a concentration of 4 ng mL−1: glucose oxidase (GOD), α-glucosidase, acid phosphatase (ACP), alkaline phosphatase (AKP), trypsin and a mixture of the above (MIX). It can be seen that even in the case of a mixture, the influence of these enzymes on the detection of trypsin can be negligible (Fig. 10 ). We attributed the high selectivity to a specific catalysis of APO. The constructed detection platform showed excellent stability, good reproducibility and high selectivity for trypsin. Above all results confirmed the feasibility of this sensor for sensitive PEC analysis.

In general, the concentration of trypsin in the serum of healthy humans is 4.1–7.4 ng mL−1 [75]. To verify the potential application value of this biosensor in clinical diagnosis of diseases, we measured different concentrations of trypsin by standard addition method in serum samples, a serum sample diluted 100 times as blank sample and the added concentrations were listed in Table 2 . The trypsin content was derived from the standard curve and the regression equation. The standard addition method was used for calculating the average recovery rate, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was obtained from a series of three samples. Specifically, a serum sample diluted 100 times was added to a PBS buffer and incubated for 1 h with different concentrations of trypsin to detect the PEC signal. As can be seen from Table 2, the recovery rate of serum samples was in the range of 97.41–103.02 %, which was very considerable in the quantitative detection of biological samples.

In this work, an ultrasensitive and simple PEC biosensor that used specific recognition for the concentration detection of trypsin in human serum was established. The construction of photoactive material NSCQDs/Fe2O3@Bi2S3 can inhibit the recombination of electron-hole pairs and promote the efficiency of electrons transferred to greatly improve the photocurrent response. APO as a signal quencher can inhibit the photocurrent response and specific hydrolysis by trypsin can enhance the photocurrent and enable the detection of trypsin. Furthermore, the PEC biosensor for trypsin detection presented a linear range from 2 to 1000 ng mL−1 and a detection limit of 0.42 ng mL−1 with good reproducibility, excellent stability and high selectivity, exhibiting a great application in human serum. The above results indicated its potential as a PEC biosensor for determination in trypsin.

Ying Zhou: Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Chenning Zhang: Resources, Supervision. Shuru Bai: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology. Jiaxue Su: Writing – review & editing. Xunyong Zhou: Resources, Supervision. Longshan Zhao: Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Youqing lift Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University [YQ202206] and the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University [ZQN2016011].
